name: St. Basil the Great
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: 4th-century bishop of Caesarea, theologian, and monastic reformer. Co-author of the Liturgy of St. Basil. Defender of Nicene orthodoxy.

name: St. Gregory Nazianzen
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: 4th-century Archbishop of Constantinople, poet-theologian. Called 'The Theologian' for his masterful orations on the Trinity.

name: St. Elizabeth Ann Seton
title: Religious
description: First native-born US citizen canonized. Founded the Sisters of Charity and established the first Catholic school in America (1810).

name: St. John Neumann
title: Bishop
description: Fourth Bishop of Philadelphia, Czech-born missionary. Built over 100 churches and established the first Catholic diocesan school system in the US.

name: St. André Bessette
title: Religious
description: Canadian Holy Cross Brother known as the 'Miracle Man of Montreal.' Built St. Joseph's Oratory on Mount Royal through faith and persistence.

name: St. Raymond of Penyafort
title: Priest
description: 13th-century Dominican friar and canon lawyer. Compiled the Decretals of Gregory IX, the authoritative collection of papal decrees.

name: St. Hilary
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: 4th-century Bishop of Poitiers, called the 'Hammer of the Arians.' Wrote extensively on the Trinity and biblical interpretation.

name: St. Anthony
title: Abbot
description: Father of Christian Monasticism (c. 251-356). Lived as a hermit in the Egyptian desert for decades, attracting disciples who formed the first monastic communities.

name: St. Agnes
title: Virgin and Martyr
description: Young Roman martyr (c. 291-304) who chose death over marriage to a pagan. One of the most venerated early martyrs; her name is in the Roman Canon.

name: St. Francis de Sales
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Bishop of Geneva, author of 'Introduction to the Devout Life.' Pioneer of spiritual direction for laypeople and co-founder of the Visitation Order.

name: Conversion of St. Paul the Apostle
title: Apostle
description: Commemorates Paul's dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus (c. 33 AD). From persecutor of Christians to the greatest missionary of the early Church.

name: St. Timothy and St. Titus
title: Bishops
description: Companions and co-workers of St. Paul. Timothy became Bishop of Ephesus; Titus became Bishop of Crete. Recipients of three Pastoral Epistles.

name: St. Thomas Aquinas
title: Priest and Doctor of the Church
description: Dominican friar (1225-1274), the 'Angelic Doctor.' Author of the Summa Theologiae. Wrote Tantum Ergo, Pange Lingua, O Salutaris Hostia, and Adoro Te Devote.

name: St. John Bosco
title: Priest
description: Italian priest (1815-1888) who dedicated his life to educating disadvantaged youth. Founded the Salesians. Pioneered the 'preventive system' of education.

name: Presentation of the Lord
description: Commemorates Mary and Joseph presenting Jesus in the Temple 40 days after his birth. Simeon recognized him as 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles.' Candlemas.

name: St. Blaise
title: Bishop and Martyr
description: Early Christian bishop martyred c. 316. Patron of throat ailments. The Blessing of Throats is traditionally celebrated on his feast day.

name: St. Ansgar
title: Bishop
description: 9th-century Benedictine monk known as the 'Apostle of the North.' Missionary bishop who brought Christianity to Denmark and Sweden.

name: St. Agatha
title: Virgin and Martyr
description: Sicilian martyr (c. 231-251) who refused a Roman governor's advances. One of seven women commemorated by name in the Roman Canon.

name: St. Paul Miki and Companions
title: Martyrs
description: Twenty-six Catholics crucified in Nagasaki, Japan in 1597. Included Jesuits, Franciscans, and Japanese laypeople. Paul Miki preached from his cross.

name: St. Scholastica
title: Virgin
description: Twin sister of St. Benedict (c. 480-543). Founded a convent near Monte Cassino. Tradition holds that her prayers caused a miraculous storm to extend her last visit with Benedict.

name: Our Lady of Lourdes
description: Commemorates the 1858 apparitions of the Blessed Virgin Mary to St. Bernadette Soubirous in Lourdes, France. World Day of the Sick.

name: Ss. Cyril and Methodius
title: Bishops
description: 9th-century brothers who evangelized the Slavic peoples. Created the Glagolitic alphabet. Co-patron saints of Europe with St. Benedict.

name: Chair of St. Peter the Apostle
description: Celebrates Peter's authority as the first Bishop of Rome. Symbolizes the unity of the Church built on the rock of Peter's confession of faith.

name: St. Polycarp
title: Bishop and Martyr
description: Bishop of Smyrna (c. 69-155), disciple of St. John the Apostle. Martyred at age 86; his account is the earliest detailed martyrdom narrative outside Scripture.

name: St. Katharine Drexel
title: Virgin
description: Philadelphia heiress (1858-1955) who gave away her fortune to serve Native Americans and African Americans. Founded the Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament and Xavier University.

name: St. Casimir
description: Lithuanian prince (1458-1484) who refused a crown and lived an ascetic life. Known for his devotion to the Eucharist and the Blessed Virgin Mary.

name: St. Perpetua and St. Felicity
title: Martyrs
description: North African martyrs (c. 203). Perpetua was a noblewoman; Felicity her slave. Their prison diary is one of the earliest Christian writings by a woman.

name: St. John of God
title: Religious
description: Portuguese soldier turned saint (1495-1550) who devoted himself to caring for the sick and poor. Founded the Brothers Hospitallers.

name: St. Frances of Rome
title: Religious
description: Roman noblewoman (1384-1440) who served the poor during plague and famine. Founded the Oblates of Mary. Known for her mystical experiences.

name: St. Patrick
title: Bishop
description: Romano-British missionary (c. 385-461) who converted Ireland to Christianity. Used the shamrock to explain the Trinity. Wrote the Confessio.

name: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: 4th-century Bishop of Jerusalem whose Catechetical Lectures are a primary source for early baptismal and eucharistic theology.

name: St. Joseph
title: Spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary
description: Foster father of Jesus, patron of the universal Church. A just man who trusted God's plan. Principal patron of workers and of a happy death.

name: Annunciation of the Lord
description: Gabriel announces to Mary that she will conceive and bear the Son of God. Mary's 'yes' — the Fiat — marks the moment of the Incarnation.

name: St. Isidore
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Archbishop of Seville (c. 560-636). Compiled the Etymologies, a 20-volume encyclopedia that preserved classical learning through the Dark Ages.

name: St. Vincent Ferrer
title: Priest
description: Spanish Dominican (1350-1419), one of the greatest preachers in Church history. Preached throughout Europe during the Western Schism.

name: St. Mark
title: Evangelist
description: Author of the earliest Gospel. Companion of Sts. Peter and Paul. Tradition holds he founded the Church in Alexandria, Egypt.

name: St. Catherine of Siena
title: Virgin and Doctor of the Church
description: Dominican mystic (1347-1380) who persuaded Pope Gregory XI to return to Rome from Avignon. Author of The Dialogue. Co-patroness of Europe and Italy.

name: St. Pius V
title: Pope
description: Dominican pope (1566-1572) who implemented the reforms of the Council of Trent, including the standardized Roman Missal and Breviary.

name: St. Joseph the Worker
description: Established by Pius XII in 1955 to honor the dignity of human labor through the example of Joseph, the carpenter who raised the Son of God.

name: St. Athanasius
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Bishop of Alexandria (c. 296-373), 'Champion of Orthodoxy.' Exiled five times for defending the divinity of Christ against Arianism.

name: Ss. Philip and James
title: Apostles
description: Philip brought Nathanael to Jesus; James 'the Less' was first bishop of Jerusalem and author of the Epistle of James.

name: St. Damien de Veuster
title: Priest
description: Belgian missionary (1840-1889) who served the leper colony on Molokai, Hawaii for 16 years. Contracted and died of leprosy himself.

name: Ss. Nereus and Achilleus
title: Martyrs
description: Roman soldiers martyred in the late 1st century. Their tomb in the Catacombs of Domitilla is among the earliest Christian burial sites in Rome.

name: Our Lady of Fatima
description: Commemorates the 1917 apparitions of the Blessed Virgin Mary to three shepherd children in Fatima, Portugal, with messages of prayer and penance.

name: St. Matthias
title: Apostle
description: Chosen by lot to replace Judas as the twelfth apostle (Acts 1:15-26). Tradition holds he preached in Judea and was martyred.

name: St. Isidore the Farmer
description: Spanish layman (c. 1070-1130) who worked as a farm laborer his entire life. Known for his devotion to daily Mass and generosity to the poor.
patronOf: farmers, rural communities, US National Rural Life Conference

name: Visitation of the Blessed Virgin Mary
description: Mary visits her cousin Elizabeth, who greets her: 'Blessed are you among women.' Mary responds with the Magnificat. Source of the Hail Mary.

name: St. Justin
title: Martyr
description: Christian philosopher (c. 100-165) whose Apologies and Dialogue with Trypho are among the earliest defenses of the faith. Gives the first detailed description of the Eucharistic liturgy.

name: Ss. Marcellinus and Peter
title: Martyrs
description: Roman priest and exorcist martyred during the Diocletian persecution (c. 304). Named in the Roman Canon of the Mass.

name: St. Charles Lwanga and Companions
title: Martyrs
description: Twenty-two Ugandan martyrs killed by King Mwanga II (1885-1887). Young men who chose death rather than submit to the king's demands. Both Catholic and Anglican.

name: St. Boniface
title: Bishop and Martyr
description: English Benedictine missionary (c. 675-754), 'Apostle to the Germans.' Organized the Church in Germany and reformed the Frankish Church.

name: St. Barnabas
title: Apostle
description: Levite from Cyprus, companion of St. Paul on his first missionary journey. His name means 'son of encouragement.' Sold his field and gave the proceeds to the apostles.

name: St. Anthony of Padua
title: Priest and Doctor of the Church
description: Portuguese Franciscan friar (1195-1231), one of the most beloved saints. Renowned preacher whose sermons drew thousands. Patron of lost things.

name: Birth of St. John the Baptist
description: The birth of the Precursor, announced by the angel Gabriel to Zechariah. Elizabeth and Zechariah's son would prepare the way for the Messiah.

name: St. Irenaeus
title: Bishop and Martyr, Doctor of the Church
description: Bishop of Lyon (c. 130-202), disciple of St. Polycarp. His 'Against Heresies' is a foundational work of Christian theology and the first systematic theology.

name: Ss. Peter and Paul
title: Apostles
description: The twin pillars of the Church: Peter, the rock on whom Christ built the Church; Paul, the apostle to the Gentiles. Both martyred in Rome under Nero.

name: St. Thomas
title: Apostle
description: Called 'Doubting Thomas' for demanding to touch Jesus' wounds, then proclaiming 'My Lord and my God!' Tradition holds he evangelized India.

name: St. Benedict
title: Abbot
description: Father of Western Monasticism (c. 480-547). His Rule of St. Benedict shaped religious life for 1,500 years. Founded Monte Cassino. Co-patron of Europe.

name: St. Kateri Tekakwitha
title: Virgin
description: Mohawk-Algonquin laywoman (1656-1680), first Native American to be canonized. Called 'Lily of the Mohawks.' Lived a life of prayer and penance.

name: St. Bonaventure
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Franciscan theologian (1221-1274), 'Seraphic Doctor.' Minister General of the Franciscans, cardinal. Authored The Journey of the Mind into God.

name: Our Lady of Mount Carmel
description: Honors the Blessed Virgin's patronage of the Carmelite Order. Tradition associates this feast with the Brown Scapular given to St. Simon Stock.

name: St. Mary Magdalene
description: Disciple of Jesus, witness to the Crucifixion, first witness of the Resurrection. Called 'Apostle to the Apostles.' Elevated to feast rank by Pope Francis in 2016.

name: St. James
title: Apostle
description: Son of Zebedee, brother of John, first apostle martyred (c. 44 AD). Pilgrimage to his shrine in Santiago de Compostela is one of the great Christian pilgrimages.

name: Ss. Joachim and Anne
title: Parents of the Blessed Virgin Mary
description: Parents of Mary and grandparents of Jesus. Though not mentioned in Scripture, their names come from the Protoevangelium of James (c. 150 AD).

name: St. Martha
description: Sister of Mary and Lazarus, friend and host of Jesus in Bethany. Professed faith in Jesus as 'the Christ, the Son of God' (John 11:27).

name: St. Ignatius of Loyola
title: Priest
description: Spanish soldier-turned-mystic (1491-1556). Founded the Society of Jesus (Jesuits). Author of the Spiritual Exercises, a cornerstone of Catholic spirituality.

name: St. Alphonsus Liguori
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Italian bishop (1696-1787), founder of the Redemptorists. Moral theologian, author of The Glories of Mary. Prolific hymn writer.

name: St. Eusebius of Vercelli
title: Bishop
description: 4th-century Bishop of Vercelli, first to unite monastic life with clerical life. Exiled for defending Nicene orthodoxy.

name: St. John Vianney
title: Priest
description: The Curé of Ars (1786-1859). Nearly failed seminary, became the most famous confessor in Church history. Spent 16-18 hours daily in the confessional.

name: Dedication of the Basilica of St. Mary Major
description: Celebrates the dedication of the oldest church in the West dedicated to the Blessed Virgin Mary, built in Rome after the Council of Ephesus (431).

name: Transfiguration of the Lord
description: Jesus reveals his divine glory to Peter, James, and John on the mountain. Moses and Elijah appear. The Father declares: 'This is my beloved Son.'

name: St. Dominic
title: Priest
description: Spanish priest (1170-1221), founder of the Order of Preachers (Dominicans). Promoted the Rosary and intellectual rigor in preaching and theology.

name: St. Teresa Benedicta of the Cross (Edith Stein)
title: Virgin and Martyr
description: Jewish philosopher who converted to Catholicism and became a Carmelite nun. Murdered at Auschwitz in 1942. Co-patroness of Europe.

name: St. Lawrence
title: Deacon and Martyr
description: Roman deacon martyred in 258. When ordered to hand over the Church's treasures, he presented the poor and sick. Tradition holds he was roasted on a gridiron.

name: St. Clare
title: Virgin
description: Follower of St. Francis of Assisi (1194-1253). Founded the Poor Clares, the second Franciscan order. Known for radical poverty and contemplative prayer.

name: St. Maximilian Kolbe
title: Priest and Martyr
description: Polish Franciscan (1894-1941) who volunteered to die in place of a stranger at Auschwitz. Founded the Militia of the Immaculata.

name: Assumption of the Blessed Virgin Mary
description: Dogma proclaimed by Pius XII in 1950: Mary was assumed body and soul into heavenly glory at the end of her earthly life. Holy day of obligation.

name: St. Stephen of Hungary
description: First King of Hungary (c. 975-1038). Converted the Magyars to Christianity and organized the Hungarian Church.

name: St. Bernard
title: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
description: Cistercian monk (1090-1153), 'Honey-Tongued Doctor.' His writings on love of God and mystical theology influenced centuries of Christian spirituality.

name: St. Pius X
title: Pope
description: Pope (1903-1914) who lowered the age of First Communion, reformed Church music (Tra le sollecitudini), and reorganized canon law.

name: Queenship of the Blessed Virgin Mary
description: Celebrates Mary's queenship over heaven and earth, established by Pius XII in 1954. Occurs one week after the Assumption.

name: St. Rose of Lima
title: Virgin
description: First saint canonized from the Americas (1586-1617). Peruvian Dominican tertiary who lived a life of severe penance and mystical prayer.

name: St. Bartholomew
title: Apostle
description: One of the Twelve Apostles, often identified with Nathanael whom Philip brought to Jesus. Tradition holds he was martyred by being flayed alive.

name: St. Monica
description: Mother of St. Augustine of Hippo (c. 331-387). Her persistent prayers for her son's conversion over many years are a model of maternal faith and intercession.

name: St. Augustine
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Bishop of Hippo (354-430), one of the most influential theologians in Western Christianity. Author of Confessions and The City of God. Convert from a dissolute youth.

name: Passion of St. John the Baptist
description: Commemorates the beheading of John the Baptist by Herod Antipas, at the request of Salome. The last and greatest of the prophets.

name: Birth of the Blessed Virgin Mary
description: Celebrates the birthday of the Mother of God. One of the few birthdays celebrated liturgically (along with Jesus and John the Baptist).

name: Most Holy Name of Mary
description: Honors the holy name of the Blessed Virgin Mary. Originally celebrated to commemorate the victory at the Battle of Vienna (1683).

name: Exaltation of the Holy Cross
description: Celebrates the finding of the True Cross by St. Helena in 326 and its recovery from the Persians in 629. The cross is the sign of victory over death.

name: Our Lady of Sorrows
description: Commemorates the Seven Sorrows of Mary, from the prophecy of Simeon to the burial of Jesus. The Stabat Mater is the traditional hymn.

name: Ss. Cornelius and Cyprian
title: Pope and Bishop, Martyrs
description: 3rd-century martyrs. Cornelius was pope; Cyprian was Bishop of Carthage. Both dealt with the question of readmitting Christians who had lapsed during persecution.

name: St. Robert Bellarmine
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Jesuit cardinal (1542-1621), chief defender of Catholic doctrine during the Reformation. His catechism was used for 300 years.

name: St. Januarius
title: Bishop and Martyr
description: Bishop of Benevento martyred during Diocletian's persecution (c. 305). Famous for the liquefaction of his blood relic in Naples Cathedral.

name: St. Matthew
title: Apostle and Evangelist
description: Tax collector called by Jesus to be one of the Twelve. Author of the first Gospel. His call narrative emphasizes that Jesus came for sinners.

name: St. Vincent de Paul
title: Priest
description: French priest (1581-1660) who organized charitable works on a massive scale. Founded the Congregation of the Mission (Vincentians) and co-founded the Daughters of Charity.

name: Ss. Michael, Gabriel, and Raphael
title: Archangels
description: The three archangels named in Scripture: Michael ('Who is like God?'), Gabriel ('God is my strength'), and Raphael ('God heals').

name: St. Jerome
title: Priest and Doctor of the Church
description: Scholar (c. 347-420) who translated the Bible into Latin (the Vulgate). Known for his fiery temperament and brilliant scholarship.

name: St. Thérèse of the Child Jesus
title: Virgin and Doctor of the Church
description: Carmelite nun (1873-1897), the 'Little Flower.' Her 'Little Way' of spiritual childhood transformed Catholic spirituality. Youngest Doctor of the Church.

name: Holy Guardian Angels
description: Celebrates the angels God assigns to guard and guide each person. Based on Jesus' words: 'Their angels in heaven always see the face of my Father.'

name: St. Francis of Assisi
description: Italian friar (1181/82-1226) who embraced radical poverty and founded the Franciscan Order. Received the stigmata. Author of the Canticle of the Sun.

name: Blessed Francis Xavier Seelos
title: Priest
description: German-born Redemptorist (1819-1867) who served as a parish priest and itinerant preacher in the United States. Died of yellow fever in New Orleans.

name: Our Lady of the Rosary
description: Celebrates the Rosary, the great Marian prayer. Originally commemorated the victory at Lepanto (1571), attributed to the Rosary's intercession.

name: St. Teresa of Jesus (Ávila)
title: Virgin and Doctor of the Church
description: Spanish Carmelite mystic (1515-1582) who reformed the Carmelite Order. Author of The Interior Castle and The Way of Perfection. First female Doctor of the Church.

name: St. Ignatius of Antioch
title: Bishop and Martyr
description: Third Bishop of Antioch (c. 35-108). His seven letters, written en route to martyrdom in Rome, are foundational documents of early Church theology and structure.
patronOf: the Church in the Eastern Mediterranean

name: St. Luke
title: Evangelist
description: Physician and companion of St. Paul. Author of the third Gospel and Acts of the Apostles. Tradition holds he painted icons of the Virgin Mary.

name: Ss. John de Brébeuf and Isaac Jogues and Companions
title: Priests and Martyrs
description: North American Martyrs: eight Jesuit missionaries killed between 1642-1649 while evangelizing the Huron and Iroquois peoples in present-day US and Canada.

name: Ss. Simon and Jude
title: Apostles
description: Simon 'the Zealot' and Jude (Thaddaeus), two of the Twelve Apostles. Jude is the author of the Epistle of Jude and patron of impossible causes.

name: All Saints
description: Celebrates all the saints, known and unknown, in heaven. Holy day of obligation. A day of hope that reminds us of our call to holiness.

name: All Souls
title: Commemoration of All the Faithful Departed
description: Day of prayer for all who have died. Three Masses may be celebrated. Honors the communion of saints and our hope in the resurrection.

name: St. Martin de Porres
title: Religious
description: Peruvian Dominican lay brother (1579-1639) of mixed African and Spanish descent. Known for his charity, humility, and miraculous healings.

name: St. Charles Borromeo
title: Bishop
description: Archbishop of Milan (1538-1584), key figure in implementing the Council of Trent. Established seminaries, catechetical schools, and personally served during plague.

name: Dedication of the Lateran Basilica
description: The cathedral of the Bishop of Rome, mother church of all churches. Dedicated in 324 under Constantine. Symbol of unity with the Holy See.

name: St. Leo the Great
title: Pope and Doctor of the Church
description: Pope (440-461) who defined Christ's two natures at the Council of Chalcedon. Persuaded Attila the Hun to turn back from Rome.

name: St. Martin of Tours
title: Bishop
description: Roman soldier who shared his cloak with a beggar (c. 316-397). Became Bishop of Tours. One of the first non-martyrs venerated as a saint.

name: St. Frances Xavier Cabrini
title: Virgin
description: Italian-American saint (1850-1917), first US citizen canonized. Founded the Missionary Sisters of the Sacred Heart and 67 institutions for immigrants.

name: St. Albert the Great
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Dominican friar (c. 1200-1280), 'Universal Doctor.' Teacher of St. Thomas Aquinas. Encyclopedic scholar of theology, philosophy, and natural science.

name: St. Margaret of Scotland
description: Queen of Scotland (c. 1045-1093) who reformed the Scottish Church and was known for her generous care of the poor and orphans.

name: St. Gertrude
title: Virgin
description: Benedictine mystic (1256-1302), author of The Herald of Divine Love. Pioneer of devotion to the Sacred Heart of Jesus.

name: St. Elizabeth of Hungary
description: Hungarian princess (1207-1231) who renounced wealth to serve the poor. Founded a hospital, cared for lepers. Franciscan tertiary.

name: Presentation of the Blessed Virgin Mary
description: Commemorates the presentation of the young Mary in the Temple. Though based on apocryphal sources, celebrates Mary's lifelong dedication to God.

name: St. Cecilia
title: Virgin and Martyr
description: Roman martyr (c. 200-230). Tradition holds she sang to God in her heart during her wedding. One of the most venerated early Roman martyrs.

name: St. Andrew Dũng-Lạc and Companions
title: Martyrs
description: 117 Vietnamese martyrs killed between 1745-1862. Includes priests, religious, and laypeople. Andrew Dũng-Lạc was a Vietnamese diocesan priest.

name: St. Andrew
title: Apostle
description: Brother of Simon Peter, first called by Jesus. Tradition holds he was crucified on an X-shaped cross. Patron of fishermen and Scotland.

name: St. Francis Xavier
title: Priest
description: Jesuit co-founder (1506-1552), greatest missionary since St. Paul. Evangelized India, Southeast Asia, and Japan. Baptized tens of thousands.

name: St. John Damascene
title: Priest and Doctor of the Church
description: Last of the Greek Fathers (c. 676-749). Defender of icons during the Iconoclast controversy. Author of 'An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.'

name: St. Nicholas
title: Bishop
description: Bishop of Myra (c. 270-343), legendary for secret gift-giving. Attended the Council of Nicaea. Inspiration for the Santa Claus tradition.

name: St. Ambrose
title: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
description: Bishop of Milan (c. 339-397) who baptized St. Augustine. Introduced antiphonal chanting to the Western Church. Powerful preacher and defender of orthodoxy.

name: Immaculate Conception of the Blessed Virgin Mary
description: Dogma that Mary was conceived without original sin. Patronal feast of the United States. Holy day of obligation.

name: St. Juan Diego Cuauhtlatoatzin
description: Indigenous Mexican (1474-1548) to whom Our Lady of Guadalupe appeared in 1531. His tilma bearing her image remains on display in Mexico City.

name: Our Lady of Guadalupe
description: Patroness of the Americas. Appeared to St. Juan Diego in 1531. Her miraculous image led to millions of conversions. Feast in the US.

name: St. Lucy
title: Virgin and Martyr
description: Sicilian martyr (c. 283-304) whose name means 'light.' Commemorated in the Roman Canon. Feast falls near the winter solstice.

name: St. John of the Cross
title: Priest and Doctor of the Church
description: Spanish Carmelite mystic (1542-1591), co-reformer of the Carmelite Order with St. Teresa of Ávila. Author of The Dark Night of the Soul.

name: St. Peter Canisius
title: Priest and Doctor of the Church
description: Dutch Jesuit (1521-1597), 'Second Apostle of Germany.' His catechisms were used for over 200 years. Tireless preacher of the Counter-Reformation.

name: St. John of Kanty
title: Priest
description: Polish priest and professor of theology (1390-1473) at the University of Kraków. Known for his generosity to the poor and students.

name: St. Stephen
title: First Martyr
description: One of the first seven deacons. First Christian martyr, stoned to death while praying for his persecutors (Acts 6-7). His feast follows Christmas.

name: St. John
title: Apostle and Evangelist
description: The 'Beloved Disciple,' son of Zebedee. Author of the fourth Gospel, three Epistles, and Revelation. The only apostle not to die a martyr.

name: Holy Innocents
title: Martyrs
description: Commemorates the infants killed by Herod in Bethlehem in his attempt to destroy the newborn King. The first martyrs for Christ.

name: St. Thomas Becket
title: Bishop and Martyr
description: Archbishop of Canterbury (1118-1170) murdered in his cathedral by knights of King Henry II. Symbol of the Church's independence from the state.

name: St. Sylvester I
title: Pope
description: Pope during Constantine's reign (314-335). Presided over the Church's transition from persecution to imperial patronage. Built the original St. Peter's Basilica.
